title: Dog Inability to Blink One Eye: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog can't blink one eye, it may be facial nerve paralysis. Learn about the medium urgency level and why photos are vital for vet triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog is unable to blink, they likely have facial nerve paralysis and require a veterinary examination within 24 hours to prevent painful corneal ulcers or permanent eye damage.

What is facial nerve paralysis in dogs and why is my pet unable to blink?

When a dog is unable to blink one eye, it is most often due to a condition called facial nerve paralysis. This nerve controls the muscles used for blinking, twitching the nose, and moving the lips. When this nerve is compromised, the eyelid remains stuck open, which prevents the eye from being naturally lubricated and protected.

Is my dog's inability to blink considered a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for an inability to blink is Medium. While this is generally not a life-threatening emergency in the immediate minutes, it requires a veterinary examination within 24 hours because:

  • The surface of the eye dries out rapidly without the protection of blinking.
  • Lack of lubrication can lead to painful corneal ulcers and scratches.
  • If left untreated, infections can develop, potentially leading to the permanent loss of the eye.

How can taking a photo of my dog's face help the veterinary team with triage?

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's face is a critical tool for your veterinary team that allows the triage nurse or doctor to:

  • Assess the degree of facial asymmetry.
  • Check for signs of ocular inflammation or redness.
  • Determine if there is any drooping of the ear or lip.
  • Decide how quickly your dog needs to be scheduled for an urgent exam based on visual evidence.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, unilateral inability to blink can result from facial paralysis, potentially caused by trauma, ear disease, or brain-stem lesions. Idiopathic facial paralysis is a common cause, particularly in breeds like Cocker Spaniels, Pembroke Welsh Corgis, and Boxers, characterized by acute onset of unilateral or bilateral paresis or paralysis of facial muscles. Clinical signs include a drooping ear, drooping upper lip, and drooling. Diagnosis is based on clinical features and exclusion of other causes. With facial paralysis, the palpebral fissure may be slightly larger on the affected side, and when the eyelids or cornea are touched, the eyelids do not close, although the eyeball will retract. Corneal irritation may lead to excessive tear production.

Chapter: Neurology, Trauma

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1259)

faqs.question: What are the common causes of a dog not blinking?
faqs.answer: Common causes include deep ear infections, trauma to the head, hypothyroidism, or idiopathic paralysis where the cause remains unknown.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for my dog in the meantime?
faqs.answer: You should never use medicated human eye drops on a dog. However, a veterinarian may recommend a simple, preservative-free artificial tear lubricant to keep the eye moist until your appointment.

faqs.question: Will my dog's ability to blink ever return?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends on the underlying cause. If the cause is an infection or underlying condition that can be treated, function may return, though some cases of nerve damage are permanent.
